title: Lists: Holding More Than One Reading
learningObjectives: Create and index a list of related readings, including with negative indices
Use slicing to extract a sub-range of readings without mutating the original list
Append to a list as new readings arrive
Explain why a list is mutable in a way a single variable's rebinding is not
whyItMatters: A single demand_kw variable can hold one reading. The AI Energy OS needs a full day of readings, or the last 24 hours of prices, before anything like forecasting is possible. Lists are the first data structure that scales past 'one value.'
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's rolling window of raw sensor readings — the direct precursor to the pandas Series used from Mission 2 onward.

problem.summary: 24 hourly readings can't reasonably be 24 separate variables.
problem.content: You now have not one demand reading but 24 of them, one per hour. A separate variable per hour (`demand_hour_0`, `demand_hour_1`, ...) doesn't scale and can't be looped over.

motivation.summary: Nearly all energy data arrives as a sequence, not a single value.
motivation.content: Every forecasting model downstream expects a sequence of values, not 24 separate named variables. Lists are how Python represents 'many of the same kind of thing,' and this is the shape nearly all energy data takes.

theory.summary: Lists are ordered and mutable; indexing, negative indexing, and slicing are the core operations.
theory.content: A list is an ordered, mutable collection: `readings = [4821, 4790, 4650]`. Indexing starts at `0`; negative indices count from the end (`readings[-1]` is the most recent reading). Slicing (`readings[0:2]`) extracts a sub-range without mutating the original list. `.append()` adds one new reading; `.extend()` adds many at once.

visualExplanation.summary: A row of numbered boxes, with append() adding one at the end and slicing highlighting a sub-range.
visualExplanation.content: A row of numbered boxes (0, 1, 2, ...) each hold one demand reading. The animation shows `.append()` adding a new box at the end, and a slice expression highlighting exactly which contiguous boxes it returns.

interactiveDemo.summary: Drag readings into an ordered row of slots, then run indexing and slicing against it.
interactiveDemo.content: Drag readings into an ordered row of slots, then run `readings[-1]` and a slice expression to see exactly which boxes get highlighted as the result.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: index, append, and slice a list of readings.

independentCoding.summary: Index, slice, and append against a list of solar readings, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Given a list of 6 solar output readings, write code that prints the first three, the last reading, and then appends one new reading you receive.

debugging.summary: Fix an off-by-one IndexError against a 4-element list.
debugging.content:
```python
readings_kw = [4821, 4790, 4650, 4700]
print(readings_kw[4])
```
This raises an `IndexError`. Explain exactly why index `4` is out of range for a 4-element list, and fix the code to safely print the last element.

reflection.summary: Growing a list doesn't require rewriting existing access code.
reflection.content: Why did adding one more reading with `.append()` not require you to rewrite any of your existing indexing code?

teachBack.summary: Explain negative indexing to someone who's only used positive indices.
teachBack.content: Explain the difference between `readings_kw[-1]` and `readings_kw[len(readings_kw)-1]` to someone who's only ever seen positive indexing.

miniProject.summary: Replace the three single readings in the mini project with a rolling list.
miniProject.content: Extend the sensor-intake script: replace the three separate single readings with a list of the last 6 hourly demand readings, and print the minimum, maximum, and most recent reading.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This rolling list is the raw input Mission 2's pandas lessons will formalize.
portfolioEvidence.content: This rolling list becomes the raw input the pandas Mission-2 lessons will convert into a proper time-indexed Series.

labs.title: Rolling Window
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise requiring correct indexing, slicing, and append() behavior against a growing list of hourly readings, including negative-index and empty-list edge cases.

simulations.title: List Index & Slice Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows a row of labeled boxes with live-highlighted indexing and slicing results as the learner edits an expression.

flashcards.front: What does readings_kw[-1] return, and why is that useful for sensor data specifically?
flashcards.back: The last element in the list — for a rolling window of readings, that's always the most recent one, without needing to know how many readings are currently in the list.

flashcards.front: Does readings_kw[1:3] modify the original list?
flashcards.back: No — slicing returns a new list containing the selected sub-range; the original list is unchanged.

rubric: Rolling window correctly uses a list rather than separate variables per reading
Correctly computes min/max/most-recent using appropriate list operations rather than hard-coded indices
Handles the append-then-reread sequence correctly, demonstrating the list is mutated in place rather than rebound
